title: The Problem With VAR at the 2026 World Cup Isn’t the Technology—It’s Who Interprets It
gist: After Egypt's controversial elimination by Argentina at the 2026 World Cup, experts interviewed by WIRED en Español argue that VAR's technology (42 cameras, semi-automated offside detection) is not the core problem — inconsistent human interpretation and uneven referee training (only 20% of participating countries use VAR domestically) are, and FIFA's 2026 rule expansions have not resolved the underlying standardization gap.

insights.claim: The Egyptian Football Association filed a formal protest with FIFA demanding an investigation into refereeing decisions after Egypt lost 3-2 to Argentina in the Round of 16 despite having held a two-goal lead, claiming failure to properly use VAR affected the result.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: A formal federation protest against VAR application at a marquee 2026 World Cup match signals ongoing institutional/reputational risk for FIFA's officiating technology program going into future tournaments.
insights.quote: the Egyptian Football Association cannot remain silent regarding the refereeing decisions observed during the match against Argentina
insights.locator: In a statement posted on social media

insights.claim: Disallowed goals for offside calls not signaled live on the field have been a recurring complaint throughout the 2026 World Cup, with some critics alleging inconsistent application that appears to benefit certain national teams.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: Perceived bias in officiating technology, even if unsubstantiated, damages trust in a governing body's flagship tech-fairness initiative — relevant to sponsors and broadcasters invested in tournament integrity.

insights.claim: FIFA's VAR system for the 2026 World Cup has access to 42 broadcast cameras (8 super-slow-motion, 4 ultra-slow-motion) plus semi-automated offside detection cameras and the full FIFA host broadcast network, reviewed by a team of one video assistant referee and three assistants.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: Quantifies the hard technology infrastructure FIFA has built around officiating — useful baseline for assessing sports-tech vendors and broadcast infrastructure investment tied to major tournaments.
insights.locator: The platform has access to 42 broadcast cameras

insights.claim: For the 2026 World Cup, FIFA expanded VAR's reviewable scenarios to include correcting clearly erroneous second yellow cards, detecting blocking/shoving/offensive fouls before free kicks or corners, and enforcing the new 'Prestianni-Vinícius Rule' penalizing players who cover their mouth with hand, arm, or jersey during confrontations.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: Rule-set expansion each cycle increases VAR's reach and the volume of subjective judgment calls funneled through officiating technology, raising rather than lowering controversy surface area at the next World Cup.
insights.locator: For this edition of the World Cup, FIFA added new situations

themes.theme: Human interpretation as the real bottleneck

insights.claim: Sports analyst Fernando Galván argues that in VAR's early years, referees increasingly delegated decisions entirely to VAR rather than using it as a support tool, and that FIFA's 2026 usage guidelines aim to restore more autonomy and judgment to the on-field referee.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: The core design tension in officiating-assist technology — tool vs. decision-maker — mirrors AI-assisted decision systems broadly; over-reliance on the tool degrades the human judgment it was meant to support.
insights.quote: First, the referee decided everything, and then VAR decided everything.

insights.claim: Former World Cup referee Armando Archundia states that VAR is not designed to eliminate error entirely; its purpose is to give the referee better information, but the final call remains an individual judgment that VAR itself will not make.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: Even the officiating technology's own design philosophy accepts persistent error and subjectivity — a caution against overselling 'AI-assisted officiating' as a fairness guarantee in any sport or decision system.
insights.quote: It is the individual judgment of each referee that makes the decision, because not even VAR is going to support them in that regard.
insights.locator: The former Mexican referee is unequivocal

insights.claim: Only about 20 percent of the countries participating in the 2026 World Cup use VAR in their domestic competitions, meaning many World Cup referees have limited real-world experience with the system despite FIFA's up-to-four-times-a-year training meetings for its pool of roughly 100 candidate referees.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: A concrete, quantified skills/experience gap explains inconsistent VAR application better than technology limitations — a transferable lesson for any organization deploying a shared assistive technology unevenly across a heterogeneous workforce.
insights.quote: only 20 percent of the countries participating in this World Cup have VAR in their domestic competitions
insights.locator: Although the selection process is governed by rigorous standards

insights.claim: Galván argues that achieving unified global refereeing criteria is 'almost impossible' because officiating is a fragmented, non-unified profession, a systemic problem evident even in regional and national tournaments, not just the World Cup.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: Standardization failures are structural/organizational, not fixable purely by adding more cameras or AI — relevant framework for any global body deploying a shared assistive technology across disparate local practices.
insights.quote: The issue with refereeing is that it's a profession that isn't very unified. That's a huge problem. Achieving unanimous criteria on a global level is almost impossible, but that also adds to the flavor of soccer.

insights.claim: There is speculation that future matches could be officiated by AI-equipped robots capable of replacing human referees for greater accuracy, though the article notes this possibility remains unconfirmed and not yet in practice.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: A speculative but notable long-horizon marker for AI/robotics vendors eyeing sports officiating as a future market, though the article treats it as unconfirmed speculation rather than a stated FIFA roadmap.

insights.claim: Archundia predicts human referees will continue to officiate soccer for the foreseeable future despite technology's expanding role, framing full automation as very distant.
insights.speaker: Fernanda González
insights.so_what: Tempers any near-term thesis around AI fully displacing human sports officials; the human-in-the-loop model looks durable per this source.
insights.quote: Refereeing and soccer will continue to be played by humans, and humans will surely continue to officiate them as well—until soccer changes its name.
